title: Should Sam Keep Working Here Next Summer?
notes:
Three-option ending. All are valid; none is 'the right one.' The one the player picks records as a canon_var on the Vol 7 gauntlet side (via GauntletState) and lands as a small dialogue callback in Chapter 15 or 16.

The game's authorial position is that all three are the right answer for different players. Ines Rocha's 2019 retrospective quote — 'I did not know, in 2003, that people would still be asking me this' — is on the ending card.

Each option's `epilogue_base` is the ending text before Act 4 has fired. After Act 4 finishes, the game selects one entry from `line_shape_riders` matching the Act 4 line-shape canon_var and appends it to the base. The rider is one sentence that ties the summer's final decision to the shape Sam drew on the beach that morning.

options.label: Yes.
options.epilogue_base: Sam stays.  The kwik stop enters its ninth summer with Sam behind the counter on the night shift.  Mr. Aandahl gets his coffee and corn nuts.  The trucker gets his six.  The kid on the bike passes by, twice a week, and does not come in.  The 2 AM customer sits at the booth every third night now, not every night; something in the estuary's second year settled.  The estuary's fisheries counts hold.  The kwik stop's cash drawer holds.  Sam holds.  The eventual owner of the shell station up the road, when it reopens under new management in 2007, will hire Sam away.  Sam will not, at that point, take the offer.  Sam stays here.  This was, all along, the correct place.

line_shape_riders.smooth: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had run clean.  It had been, in retrospect, the same shape as the answer.
line_shape_riders.jagged: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had been a jagged one and had not, in retrospect, been the shape of the answer.  Sam stayed anyway.  Sometimes the line and the answer do not agree, and one of them turns out to be lying, and it is not always the answer.
line_shape_riders.returned: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had gone out and come back.  Twice.  Sam had not, at 7:14 AM, had a word for the shape.  In September of 2007 Sam would find the word.  Sam would not, at that point, tell anyone.
line_shape_riders.unfinished: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had been a short one.  Sam had walked back up to the parking lot before the tide reached it.  Which was, in retrospect, a way of saying: not this morning, not the whole answer, not yet.  Sam stayed anyway.  The whole answer came later, in pieces.
line_shape_riders.fallback: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning was the line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning.  The tide took it.  Sam signed it, or Sam didn't.  Either way, Sam stayed.

options.label: No.
options.epilogue_base: Sam finishes the shift on the second-spring morning and hands the keys to Jules at 6:14 AM.  Jules says nothing.  Sam drives up the coast highway to Astoria and gets a job at a bookstore that will close in nine months but not before Sam meets someone at the register there whose name they will remember for the rest of their life.  The kwik stop hires a night clerk from Portland who lasts three weeks.  The estuary's fisheries counts continue to hold; the tide gate is not, at that point, Sam's job to open.  It was somebody's job.  Now it is somebody else's.

line_shape_riders.smooth: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had run clean and the heron had walked alongside it.  Sam drove north with the specific weightless feeling of having drawn a clean thing before leaving.  The feeling did not last all the way to Astoria.  Some of it did.
line_shape_riders.jagged: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had been jagged and the crab had come to three of the sharp corners.  Sam had looked at the finished shape from the top of the dune trail and thought · yes.  That is the shape.  Sam drove north two hours later.
line_shape_riders.returned: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had gone out and come back and, at the crossing point, had made a shape Sam could not name.  The shape stayed with Sam.  Sam thought about the shape, on Wednesday mornings, for the next three years.  Sam never drew it again.
line_shape_riders.unfinished: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning had been a short one.  Sam had set the stick down and walked back up the beach before the tide reached the drawing.  Which was, in a way, already the leaving.  Sam started driving north a few hours later.
line_shape_riders.fallback: The line Sam drew on the beach that Tuesday morning was drawn and the tide took it.  Sam drove north.  Sam did not, on the drive, replay the line in Sam's head.  The line had, at 7:14 AM, released Sam.

options.label: The question is wrong.
options.epilogue_base:
The screen prints the words 'THE QUESTION IS WRONG.' and holds them for six seconds.  Then, in a smaller font: 'You have been asked, this whole time, whether Sam should keep working at the kwik stop.  You have been shown the kwik stop and the estuary and the tide gate and the sedge wren and the twenty-two nights the 2 AM customer sat in the corner booth.  You have run the summer twice.  The question the game meant to ask, and did not, is whether the kwik stop should keep being here.  Because a kwik stop that pays the mortgage on the cabin the current player's uncle built in 1979 is not a kwik stop.  It is the reason the cabin still exists.  It is the estuary's fourth species after coho, sturgeon, and goby: the small commercial establishment that sits at coordinate (384, 210) in the top right of the map.  Sam does not, in this ending, matter.  The kwik stop matters, and it stays.'

The game returns to the title screen.  The title screen has, on the third playthrough, an additional menu option labeled 'about this game.' It contains Ines Rocha's 2019 retrospective and the 2003 developer commentary that the studio did not, at the time, publish.

line_shape_riders.smooth: 'On the beach that Tuesday morning, at 7:14 AM, Sam drew a line that ran clean.  The tide took it.  This game recorded, on the cartridge sleeve, that the line ran clean.  The next time you boot this game the sleeve will remember.  Which is, in the technical language of the design, all that the game means by an ending.'
line_shape_riders.jagged: 'On the beach that Tuesday morning, at 7:14 AM, Sam drew a jagged line.  The crab came to three of the corners.  The line, when the tide took it, was legibly the letter you did not intend to draw.  This game recorded, on the cartridge sleeve, which letter.  We will not, in this program, tell you which.  You will find it, if you find it, the eleventh time you turn the game on.'
line_shape_riders.returned: 'On the beach that Tuesday morning, at 7:14 AM, the line Sam drew went out and came back and at its center was a shape Sam did not have a word for.  You, the player, have already given us a name for this shape by playing the game the way you played it.  The name is not a name that a design document can print.  The cartridge sleeve has recorded it.  It will surface, on the twenty-seventh boot, when the game does not, that time, ask you the ending question.'
line_shape_riders.unfinished: 'On the beach that Tuesday morning Sam did not finish the line.  This is the ending the game has been waiting for.  It is not sad.  It is the answer the game did not know it was going to accept.  The cartridge sleeve records: LINE UNFINISHED.  Which, in Oneironautics Inc.'s internal engineering language, is a state we in the studio call OPEN.  You have left the game open.  It will be open the next time.'
line_shape_riders.fallback: 'On the beach that Tuesday morning, at 7:14 AM, Sam drew a line and the tide took it.  The line was what the line was.  This game recorded, on the cartridge sleeve, what you did.  We will not tell you what was recorded.  The recording is between you and the sleeve.  It has been between the two of you all along.'

runtime_notes.epilogue_composition: The Godot Estuary3Ending.gd host reads: (1) `options[picked].epilogue_base` and (2) `options[picked].line_shape_riders[canon_vars.estuary_3_line_shape]`, then renders base + '\n\n' + rider.  If the line-shape canon_var is unset for any reason (e.g. player quit mid-Act-4), the host falls back to the 'fallback' rider entry.
runtime_notes.canon_var_composition: Both the option's canon_var and the Act 4 line-shape canon_var write to GauntletState.state.canon_vars.  Both lore_tokens write to GauntletState.state.lore_tokens_revealed.  slowsticks_finished appends 'estuary_3'.

post_ending_screen.title: ESTUARY 3 · CREDITS
credits: ADDITIONAL DESIGN · The Oneironautics Kwik-Stop Team, whose failed 2003 project became this game's first half
PROGRAMMING · Ines Rocha · Marc Ostrom · Sarah Delahaye
PRODUCED BY · Oneironautics Inc., Portland, Oregon

IN MEMORY OF · the actual kwik stop on Highway 202 outside of Smolvud, Oregon, which closed in 2001 and was, when the studio drove past it in 2003, the visual reference for this game's first half.  It has since reopened under new ownership and is, in 2019, still there.
'I did not know, in 2003, that people would still be asking me this.'  — Ines Rocha, 2019
